I used the small, black five star g-whiz twice in 48 hours. I rinsed the blades off with tap water. Next morning, the blades had rust on them. I got a full refund by Amazon since I bought it directly from them. End of story.

Wahl customer support, I want to buy and like your products, but now I've got to ask which of your products do not have this problem. I just want a cordless battery powered trimmer that won't rust. As sales and business owners, it really doesn't make economic sense to sell something with metal in it guaranteed to rust as soon as it hits water, since people are going to do a 180 and get a refund.

Maybe the metal does have the benefit of being easier to machine and result in higher quality blades, but it's really not an option to have easily oxidized metal in a product that gets washed frequently.

I'm open to buying Wahl trimmers again, but I need recommendations for which models won't have fundamental engineering design flaws. I'll spend the money, just tell me what actually works.

P.S. the trimmer works fine otherwise. Maybe a little wimpy in terms of power output, but no hair pulling. Pretty noisy, but who cares. Just lubricate it properly. It's smaller than the Remington MB4900, but the blade width is almost as long. It's lightweight and very small, so I guess if you have gigantic man-hands it might not offer the best ergonomics. I actually prefer non-permanent batteries, so that was a plus for me. The trimmer heads aren't easily removable for cleaning. I don't think the body is very waterproof (aside from the obvious aforementioned blade rusting), so be wary about using it in the shower.

Brush + Case + Sponge:
The Kabuki brush is a little bit long for my taste for being a travel size brush, but it is still much smaller than a lot of other travel size kabuki brushes on the market. The case is cute but a little bit heavy. I hope in the future the company will consider making it lighter and thus even more portable. Some people might like the substantial feel though. The sponge (is it a sponge?) that came with the case is pretty confusing, as it is not a conventional size nor texture for these sorts of makeup. I don't use it as intended. Instead, I placed it directly on top of the foundation to serve as a cushion when I'm moving around to prevent the foundation from shattering.

Foundation:
The foundation itself is pretty great. It doesn't feel grainy on skin and helps even out my complexion without drying my skin. I have light to medium Asian skin tone, and the shade Supernatural works just fine. It is slightly lighter than my skin tone when I'm tanned, but I only apply it around the center of my face anyways, so there is less concern about blending to the neck to begin with. I might try a shade darker if I get tanner in the future.

I use a brush to apply the foundation and have it settle into my pores before I apply another layer on top with my own sponge (purchased from Muji) to give more coverage if needed. When I'm on the go, skipping the brush step is usually fine. I can sort of squeeze my sponge into the designated slot.
